review_text: I see this film a bit differently than most. The attraction for me is not the exposé of racial injustice in the South because that is well-trodden ground. I was much more intrigued by our understanding and MISunderstanding of mental illness, and the innocence of the children as they are thrust into complexities of a grown-up world. Oh, and the score is wonderful.

Ambientado no sul dos Estados Unidos, durante a década de 1930, uma época fortemente época marcada não apenas pela Grande Depressão, mas também pela segregação racial, esse drama jurídico é a verdadeira definição de um clássico atemporal, pois seu peso artístico é tão importante quanto a sua contribuição social. Ao mesmo tempo que dá uma aula de narrativa cinematográfica, o filme aborda questões sociais que seguem pertinentes até hoje, mais de 60 anos após seu lançamento. Mais que um título incontornável para os amantes da 7ª arte, "O Sol é para Todos" deveria ser leitura obrigatória nas escolas e quaisquer outros ambientes em que sociedade e huminadade são objetos de estudo, discussão e aprendizado.

If your only exposure to To Kill a Mockingbird was being forced to read and/or watch it in school, you should watch it again by choice. This is a powerful film. It also manages to convey a moral message without coming across as heavy-handed. The cinematography is simple, but that gives the story and the acting a chance to shine. Gregory Peck portrays Atticus Finch with a quiet dignity that I hope to emulate in my life, and the child actors both give performances that feel completely natural. To Kill a Mockingbird is a well-crafted film adaptation of one of the most iconic American novels of all time.
This movie is an illustration of how to effectively adapt a book for the screen.

I haven't watched this or read this in a long time and wanted to check it out again. The story/plot is well written and shocking/sad and for the time, this movie has good acting. The music and cinematography, camera work, and directing are all great as well. It even comes off as a creepy film every now and then which I actually enjoyed. I was surprised by Robert Duvall's performance and character, he wasn't in the movie much and didn't have any lines at all but his presence on screen felt special for some reason and was placed well at the end. There were a couple of slower scenes in the movie that could've been cut out in my opinion, however that definitely didn't ruin it. I can see why this has a high rating!

a very beautiful story i think it has to be a must watch, and a must read book, for everyone. the innocence of childhood, prejudices and racism are just a few of the topics this marvelous story guide us through. scout and jem are two precious children of the one and only, the lawyer supreme, atticus finch, who teach his son and daugther, as well as the audience and the entirety county of maycomb, how a man is not just guilty because of the fact that he's black, and how people often abuse their power in order to cause trouble to others less fortunated or in a different position/status. i read the book prior to watching the film, as even though things don't happen exactly as they did in between the pages, the main storyline is there and the characters are portrayed to perfection. literally i can't imagine a different cast for the movie. it's one of the first ones, not to say the first black and white movie i've watched from start to finish in my life, so it'll always be extra special for that as well. i though that because it was old and a film noir i wouldn't like it or appreciated as much. let me tell myself that this factor was a key part of the story (being somewhat focused on race and privilege) and i think that made it work better because i don't think i would have liked it if it was full on color. plus, the whole format of it all was incredible to experience, so maybe i'll start watching more of those in the future. the story, being told from the perspective of scout, a girl in the midst of this whole problem, is just amazing. as i said, it combines the innocence of a child with problems that concerned the adults of the society, narrated by the protagonist grown up! the movie felt like a dream after reading the book, being a proud adaptation of it all and demonstrating how good words can become an experience. everyone should know this story and film, and even though i don't consider it one of my favorites because i mainly find others better, i think it's important to share this type of content more often in these days when discrimination is sadly still a problem!!! some things haven't changed since the 1930s and i think it's time to change that <3

An All Time Classic. The only issue I have is while the pacing is very good I do feel some of the first half could have been changed to make it flow more cohesively as this is very heavy with symbolism especially in the first half that isn't inherently obvious especially to someone who isn't familiar with the novel. This is here this is unique because 99% of kids read the novel first in school and than watch this so it isn't as big of a issue beforehand. However, once we get to the Mob outside of the jail and Atticus makes a stand is where this never lets up from there with gripping drama. Everything else though including a surprisingly catchy and moving soundtrack is incredibly done. The performances of Scout & Atticus are incredible and Robert Duvall gives a incredible performance never uttering a single word and is just as memorable. Watching this made me realize Boo represents innocence as well and isn't something I picked up from the book. This film despite its subject matter never comes across once as cynical, but very innocent and pure just like the black and white it is shot in. Even if your not familiar with the novel this is a extremely well done and very faithful adaptation. Everyone should see this once, its very powerful and moving despite taking its time at a leisurely pace.
Everyone should see this at least once.

Now and then there is a movie that does more than just represent humanity it shapes it. This one has been out there for a while on a must-see movie, but I never understood it beyond it being Superman's favorite movie. It took me a while to make time for it and in the end, it was well worth it. Scout and Jem Finch are two kids out enjoying their young lives in rural Depression-era Alabama. Like most innocent kids of their time, they learn the ways of the world while just having fun and being raised by their father, a lawyer with impeccable quality for right and wrong given where he lives as he ends up being put into a trial that teaches his children a great deal regarding the lack of humanity for black people in the South. Atticus Finch defends Tom Robinson, a black man accused of raping a young white woman. Despite the idea that an accused is innocent until proven guilty, it is obvious that Tom Robinson is innocent, and his only crime is that he is a black man desired by a white woman he had spurned. That alone in the white rural South is despised and disgusted by the ignorant and hateful people of this area. Justice is served if one could call it that as the Finch family live and learn with such honesty and righteousness. I watched this and loved it with all my heart. With great acting from not just Academy Award-winning Gregory Peck but also the kids who show that talent is not just limited to adults. But it is the story that earns its place as a timeless treasure that should be seen by all ages.
